headline: Week’s Best: SpaceX Is Changing Market Indexes. ETF Investors, Take Note.
summary: In the pre-  SpaceX  days, it didn’t really matter which major index benchmark an investor chose, whether it was constructed by Standard & Poor’s, the Center for Research in Security Prices (CRSP), or FTSE Russell.  Results of exchange-traded funds based on indexes could rise or fall based on whether they include SpaceX.  Retirement investors need to account for this new risk, writes guest columnist Daniel P. Wiener, founder and former chairman of RWA Wealth Partners.

headline: Once JPMorgan’s Top Trader, He’s Now a Favorite to Succeed Jamie Dimon as CEO
summary: Over two decades at JPMorgan, Troy Rohrbaugh earned a reputation as a shrewd risk manager. Now he’ll go from investing banking to running the bank’s sprawling consumer operation.

headline: How Citigroup's Stress Test Success Strengthens Shareholder Returns
summary: C's 2026 Fed stress test success boosts capital flexibility, supporting higher dividend payments, a $30B buyback and long-term shareholder returns.

headline: Truist Securities Maintains Buy on Wells Fargo, Raises Price Target to $94
summary: Truist Securities  analyst John McDonald   maintains Wells Fargo (NYSE:WFC) with a Buy and raises the price target from $90 to $94.

headline: Capital Deterioration Projected In Stress Test Falls To Lowest Level In 7 Years
summary: Projected losses dropped for the second year in a row in this year's Federal Reserve stress test compared to the year prior, to the lowest level in at least seven years. Read more here.

headline: The Bull Case For Wells Fargo (WFC) Could Change Following Fed Stress Test And Dividend Hike Plan
summary: Earlier this week, Wells Fargo & Company cleared the Federal Reserve’s 2026 stress test with its stress capital buffer unchanged at 2.5% and stated it expects to lift its third-quarter 2026 common dividend by 11% to US$0.50 per share, pending board approval in July. This combination of a clean bill of health from regulators and a planned cash return increase underscores Wells Fargo’s current capital strength and confidence in its balance sheet resilience.
